concerns.text: The plan explicitly acknowledges the study is 'observational' and claims will be 'strictly correlational' (research.md), yet the spec (US-3) and plan summary demand predicting 'transition probability' and validating 'transition trajectories' as if the model captures causal dynamics. A correlational design cannot validate a model's ability to predict the *timing* of a physiological event before it occurs without ruling out that the model is simply learning the annotation boundary artifact. The methodology must explicitly address how the model distinguishes pre-transition physiological precursors from the post-transition state reflected in the 60s window labels.

The plan proposes using 'Cluster-Based Permutation Tests' on extracted features to identify significant differences, then training a 1D-CNN on those same features. This creates a methodological circularity: if the statistical test fails to find significant differences (due to low power in ~40 subjects), the feature set for the CNN is undefined. The plan lacks a fallback mechanism or a unified modeling approach (e.g., end-to-end feature learning) that does not rely on a prior univariate statistical filter which may be underpowered.

The power analysis admits a limitation with ~40 subjects but proceeds to train a deep learning model (1D-CNN) on this small dataset. While the model is 'lightweight' (100k params), training a neural network on ~10k epochs from only ~40 subjects with a subject-level split risks severe overfitting or failure to converge to a generalizable solution. The methodology does not propose regularization strategies (e.g., dropout, weight decay, data augmentation) or a specific cross-validation scheme (e.g., Leave-One-Subject-Out) required to validate generalizability on such a small N.
